items.mistake: Calling any large train–validation gap 'high variance' without checking bias
items.fix: Inspect both training and validation metrics and learning curves; high bias (both scores poor) differs from high variance (training strong, validation weak).
items.why: Misdiagnosis leads to wrong fixes—adding regularization when the model is already underfitting wastes capacity.
items.example: Linear model with 55% train and 54% val accuracy is likely high bias, not high variance; a 99% train / 70% val tree is classic high variance.

items.mistake: Confusing Var(X) of a random variable with Var(ŷ) in the bias–variance sense
items.fix: Var(X) in probability measures spread of outcomes; Var(ŷ) measures spread of predictions across training sets. See Expectation & Variance for the former; this topic for the latter.
items.why: Mixing definitions causes incorrect interview answers and wrong intuition about feature variance vs model variance.
items.example: Standardizing a feature to unit Var(X) does not by itself guarantee low Var(ŷ) of your classifier.

items.mistake: Assuming more parameters always mean higher variance
items.fix: Variance depends on effective capacity, data size, and regularization—not parameter count alone. Large regularized models on big data can generalize well.
items.why: Over-simplifying pushes teams to underpower models that would generalize with proper regularization and data.
items.example: A wide neural net with dropout and weight decay can outperform a shallow net on ImageNet despite many more parameters.

items.mistake: Tuning only on training accuracy when variance is the problem
items.fix: Use hold-out validation, cross-validation, or learning curves; prioritize validation metric and stability across folds.
items.why: Training score improves monotonically as variance rises—you optimize the wrong objective.
items.example: Increasing tree depth from 5 to 30 raises training accuracy from 92% to 100% while validation falls from 88% to 83%.
